title: Methodologies and Procedures Employed in the Identification and Quantitation of Lipid Mediators via LC-MS/MS
description: {"blocks":[{"key":"odxja","text":"Liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) is a sensitive and robust approach for the identification and quantitation of lipid mediators including the specialized pro-resolving mediators (resolvins, maresins, protectins and lipoxins), and the classic eicosanoids. Due to the relatively low concentration of these molecules in biological samples, this methodology is classically coupled with solid phase extraction (SPE) techniques that enhance the sensitivity of the assay. Herein, we provide a step-by-step description of instrumentation employed in the both SPE and LC-MS/MS and detailed methodologies employed in the extraction of these lipid mediators and their identification and quantitation using LC-MS/MS.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"jemnu","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"nt8ve","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"cscrm","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}
guidelines: {"blocks":[{"key":"uvqru","text":"Lipid mediators play a central role in the regulation of a whole range of biological functions in both in humans and experimental systems 1, 2, 3, 4. These molecules are produced in a range of organs and tissues at concentrations that are commensurate with their biological actions, which in large part are dictated by the affinity of these molecules to their cognate receptors. Liquid chromatography tandem mass spectrometry (LC-MS/MS) represents a robust and highly sensitive approach for both the identification and quantitation of these potent autacoids since chromatography allows for the separation of isobaric molecules as well as double bond and chiral isomers. Mass spectrometry captures information on the fragmentation profile of the molecules of interest, which coupled with the retention time of the molecule in liquid chromatography provides a fingerprint for the identification of these molecules. In addition, LC-MS/MS allows for the simultaneous monitoring of tens and sometimes hundreds of molecules providing a snapshot of a number of dynamic pathways and the flux down each of these bioactive pathways. Herein, we shall review the protocols employed to obtain a snapshot of the dynamic pathways for the four major bioactive metabolomes that include the arachidonic acid, eicosapentaenoic acid, n-3 docosapentaenoic acid and docosahexaenoic acid metabolomes 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11, 12. These approaches help providing insights into mechanisms activated during inflammation as well as new leads into underlying causes of disease by measuring the flux down each of the major bioactive metabolomes 5, 6, 7, 8, 9, 10, 11. Given that the production and many of the biological actions of these mediators are conserved throughout evolution, these methodologies are applicable to biological material from experimental systems such as tunicates 13, mice 5, 7 and baboons 9 as well as humans 6, 8, 11, 14, 15. Thus, these methodologies facilitate the direct translation of findings made in experimental systems to humans and vice versa.","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[{"style":"italic","offset":2033,"length":10},{"style":"sup","offset":138,"length":10},{"style":"sup","offset":1377,"length":25},{"style":"sup","offset":1613,"length":21},{"style":"sup","offset":1854,"length":2},{"style":"sup","offset":1863,"length":4},{"style":"sup","offset":1880,"length":1},{"style":"sup","offset":1900,"length":16}],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"od5op","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}},{"key":"hnfci","text":"","type":"unstyled","depth":0,"inlineStyleRanges":[],"entityRanges":[],"data":{}}],"entityMap":{}}

steps: See Figure 1 for schematic representation
Blood was collected with anti-coagulant for plasma and centrifuged human peripheral blood at 1500 x g for 10 min at room temperature
Collect plasma
Store at -80ºC until analysis
Allow the sample to gently thaw on ice

Prepare methanol containing internal standard (methanol-IS; 500pg in 2mL of methanol/0.5mL of plasma) to facilitate quantification (d 4 -PGE 2 , d 5 -RvD2, d 5 -LXA 4 , d 4 -LTB 4 , d 8 -5S-HETE, d 5 -LTC 4 , d 5 -LTD 4 and d 5 -LTE 4 )
Place methanol-IS on ice for 45 min
Put 1mL of plasma in a 15 mL falcon tube
Add 4 mL of cold methanol-IS
Vortex for 10 sec
Keep samples at -20°C for a minimum of 45 min to allow for protein precipitation
Samples can either be stored -80ºC or processed for lipid mediator extraction using solid phase extraction techniques
Centrifuge samples at 2000 x g for 10 min at 4°C
Transfer 2.5 mL of supernatant into round bottom borosilicate tubes
Store any remaining samples at -80ºC
Place samples in TurboVap LV setting the water bath to 37°C under a gentle flow of Nitrogen gas
Maintain samples in the evaporator until the final volume of methanol is less than 1mL
Place samples on ice Critical: Samples should not be stored and need to be processed ASAP
Turn on and set-up Extrahera
Add new Solid-phase ISOLUTE C18 500 mg/3 mL columns to the rack
Add new collection tubes
Fill the solvent stock bottles
Prime the different solvent feeders
Turn on the vacuum pump (Adjust the pressure in accordance with the number of samples you plan to run: 3 bar = ¼ , 4 bar = ½ and 6 bars = full plate)
Turn on the nitrogen supply (Adjust the pressure in accordance with the number of samples you plan to run: 6 bar = ¼, 8 bar = ½ and 10 bars = full plate)
Empty the waste
Fill the tips
Load the samples on Extrahera and start the run
Wash columns with 3 mL methanol
Condition columns with 3 mL of water
Acidify samples by adding pH 3.5 water (HCl) to a final volume of 10 mL
Load samples onto the C18 SPE columns
Wash columns with 2 mL of water to remove the acid
Wash columns with 6 mL hexane to remove complex lipids
Elute mediators into collection tubes using 5 mL methyl formate
Elute conjugated mediators into separate collection tubes using 5 mL methanol
Transfer the collected fractions to 15 mL falcon tubes
Rinse the collection tubes with 1.5 mL methanol and pool with the respective fraction
Place samples in TurboVap LV setting the water bath to 37°C under a gentle flow of Nitrogen gas
Place an equal volume to the extracted sample of methanol-IS into a separate 15 mL falcon tube and place it in evaporator with samples. This will be used as a reference to calculate sample recovery
Check regularly the solvent levels
When ~95% of the solvent has evaporated, rinse the wall with 2 mL methyl formate
Check regularly the solvent levels
When ~95% is evaporated, rinse the wall with 2 mL methanol
Leave samples in TurboVap LV until all the solvent has evaporated
Add 40 μL of methanol/water (1:1, vol/vol)
Vortex 10 sec
Immediately centrifuge samples at 2000 x g for 2 min at 4°C
Transfer the supernatant into borosilicate vial inserts placed in a 1.5 mL Eppendorf tubes
Centrifuge at 10000 x g for 10 sec at 4°C
Check the vial insert for the presence of pellet
If no pellet is visible, transfer the insert into an injection tube
If a pellet is visible, repeat step 33-35
Place the samples in the automated injector ASAP
Start the LC-MS/MS system
Methyl formate fraction profiling instrument setup An Agilent Poroshell 120 EC-C18 column (100 mm x 4.6 mm x 2.7 µm) is kept at 50°C
The initial mobile phase consists of methanol/water/acetic acid 20:80:0.01 (vol/vol/vol)
The flow rate is maintained at 0.5 mL/min
The mobile phase is ramped to 50:50:0.01 (vol/vol/vol) over 0.5 min
The mobile phase is ramped to 80:20:0.01 (vol/vol/vol) from 2 min to 11 min
The mobile phase is maintained till 14.5 min
The mobile phase is ramped to 98:2:0.01 (vol/vol/vol) for the next 0.1 min
The mobile phase is maintained at 98:2:0.01 (vol/vol/vol) for 5.4 min
Inject 35 µL of the sample
Include in the sequence reference standards and the 100% IS (40 µL injection)
Methanol fraction profiling instrument setup An Agilent Poroshell 120 EC-C18 column (100 mm x 4.6 mm x 2.7 µm) is kept at 50°C
The initial mobile phase consists of methanol/water/acetic acid of 20:80:0.5 (vol/vol/vol)
The flow rate is maintained at 0.6 mL/min
The mobile phase is ramped to 55:45:0.5 (vol/vol/vol) over 0.2 min
The mobile phase is maintained for 1 min
The mobile phase is ramped to 70:30:0.5 (vol/vol/vol) over 5min
The mobile phase is ramped to 80:20:0.5 (vol/vol/vol) for 2 min
The mobile phase is maintained for 3 min
The mobile phase is ramped to 98:2:0.5 (vol/vol/vol) over 3 min
The mobile phase is maintained for 2 min
Inject 35 µL of the sample
Include in the sequence reference standards and the 100% IS (40 µL injection)
Chiral LC-MS/MS A Chiralpak AD-RH column (150 mm × 2.1 mm × 5 μm) is kept at RT
The mobile phase consists of methanol/water/acetic acid 95:5:0.01 (v/v/v)
The flow rate is maintained at 0.15 mL/min
The mobile phase is maintained for 15 min
Inject no more than 10 µL of sample
Include in the sequence reference standards and the 100% IS
Multiple reaction monitoring (MRM) method setup for mediators eluted in the methyl formate fraction QTrap 5500 or QTrap 6500+ are operated in negative mode

Source parameters (probe position, electrode position, curtain gas, collisionally activated dissociation gas, electrode voltage, source temperature, and source gas) are determined using manual tune tool from analyst software by T-infusion of synthetic or authentic standards in phase (see Table 1 and 2 for parameters used)

Individual MRM transitions (Q1 and Q3) and respective parameters (declustering potential, entrance potential, collision energy and collision cell exit potential) are determined using manual tune tool from analyst software by T-infusion of synthetic or authentic standards in phase (see Table 3 and 4 for parameters used)
Select Scheduled MRM option
Set data acquisition window to 90 seconds
MRM for method setup for mediators eluted in the methanol fraction QTrap 5500 or QTrap 6500+ are operated in positive mode

Source parameters (probe position, electrode position, curtain gas, collisionally activated dissociation gas, electrode voltage, source temperature, and source gas) are determined using manual tune tool from analyst software by T-infusion of synthetic or authentic standards in phase (see Table 5 and 6 for parameters used)

Individual MRM transitions (Q1 and Q3) and respective parameters (declustering potential, entrance potential, collision energy and collision cell exit potential) are determined using manual tune tool from analyst software by T-infusion of synthetic or authentic standards in phase (see Table 3 and 4 for parameters used)
MRM for chiral analysis: QTrap 5500 or QTrap 6500+ are operated in negative mode

Source parameters (probe position, electrode position, curtain gas, collisionally activated dissociation gas, electrode voltage, source temperature, and source gas) are determined using manual tune tool from analyst software by T-infusion of synthetic or authentic standards in phase (see Table 1 and 2 for parameters used)

Individual MRM transitions (Q1 and Q3) and respective parameters (declustering potential, entrance potential, collision energy and collision cell exit potential) are determined using manual tune tool from analyst software by T-infusion of synthetic or authentic standards in phase (see Table 3 and 4 for parameters used)
Information Dependent Acquisition (IDA)-triggered Enhanced Product Ion (EPI) Scan To collect MS/MS spectral information from the same samples an IDA experiment is to be employed
In the IDA Ion exclusion tab list the parent ions of the internal standards that are being used for the analysis to minimize interference from these molecules in data collection
Set the polarity of the assay in the EPI scan window as appropriate
Set the m/z window for ion detection in the EPI window to 100-400 for negative ion mode experiments and 100-680 for positive ion mode experiments
The entire analysis is conducted using Analyst software 1.6.3
Build a quantitation method using your standard reference mix
Load your samples using the created method
Integrate the internal standards noting the retention times of each internal standard in your samples and standards
Identify each LM by matching its retention time to synthetic or authentic standards with maximum drift between the expected retention time and the observed retention time window of 0.05 seconds

Each internal standard represents specific region of the chromatographic profile. Use: d 4 -PGE 2 for AA-derived prostaglandins and thromboxane d 5 -RvD2 and d 5 -LXA 4 for trihydroxylated species DHA/DPA and AA/EPA-derived respectively d 4 -LTB 4 for dihydroxylated species DHA/DPA/AA/EPA-derived d 8 -5S-HETE for monohydroxylated species DHA/DPA/AA/EPA-derived d 5 -LTC 4 for glutathionyl conjugated species DHA/AA-derived d 5 -LTD 4 for cysteinylglycinyl conjugated species DHA/AA-derived d 5 -LTE 4 for cysteinyl conjugated species DHA/AA-derived

Internal standards are used to: calculate the expected RT for the analytes of interest in each sample by comparing the relative retention time of the analyte with the respective internal standard in the standard mix. calculate recoveries, comparing the amount of each of the internal standards present in the samples with the amount added, as calculated from the 100% sample.
Verify that each peak has at least 4 data points and > 2000 counts
Integrate each LM

Using the Analyst Explore mode extract the EPI data and for each dataset, for each mediator, in a minimum of one sample per dataset and within the same region of the chromatogram where the peak is observed, match the MS/MS spectrum to that of a reference standard. For a positive match the MS/MS spectrum needs to contain a minimum of 6 diagnostic ions that match those assigned within the reference MS/MS spectrum, with one of these being a backbone fragment.
Proceed to quantitation using calibration curves obtained for each mediator construed with the following mediator concentrations: 0 (blank) 0.78, 1.56, 3.12, 6.25, 12.5, 25, 50, 100 and 200 pg.
Surrogate molecules carrying similar physical properties are used for molecules where standards are not available

1. To avoid isomerization of standards all stocks should be stored under nitrogen, shielded from light and at -20 o C for short-term storage and -80 o C for long-term storage. 2. To minimize oxidation of lipid mediators when stocks are used the head space in the vial should be purged using a gentle stream of nitrogen very briefly before closing. 3. To ensure accuracy when aliquoting lipid mediator stocks zero dead volume, Hamilton syringes should be used at all times. This should be thoroughly cleaned with isopropanol and methanol using a minimum of 20 syringe volumes of each solvent before and after using to avoid cross contamination 4. Only use nitrogen or an inert gas to evaporate solvents, do not use air since this will lead to mediator oxidation. 5. Once samples are suspended in water/methanol, these need to be kept at 4ºC until injection into the LC-MS/MS 24h to avoid isomerization of the mediators. Samples should not be frozen to minimize mediator isomerization

For 24 samples: 1) Sample thawing and protein precipitation - ~2-4h 2) Automated extraction - ~2.5h 3) Solvent evaporation and sample preparation for LC-MS/MS - ~3-4h 4) LC-MS/MS data acquisition - ~18h 4) Data analysis - ~1-2 days

This methodology will yield a snapshot of the flux down each of the arachidonic acid, eicosapentaenoic acid, n-3 docosapentaenoic acid and docosahexaenoic acid metabolomes in the biological sample of interest. Thus, providing insights into the inflammation-resolution status of the biological sample. It will also yield information on lipid mediator biosynthetic enzyme activity.
Figure 1. Illustrative overview of the methodologies employed for lipid mediator extraction, identification and quantitation
Tables 1-6.Pdf Tables 1-6
